text: The robotic right colectomy is performed for cancer treatment, featuring intracorporeal and serine anastomosis in a patient with situs inversus totalis, where the anatomical structures are mirrored.
Situs inversus totalis is a rare anatomical anomaly where organs are disposed in a reversal of the usual position.
It occurs, one, every five thousand to twenty thousand people.
Twenty-two patients with situs inversus totalis who underwent colorectal minimally invasive surgery
were reported in literature in the last eighteen years.
A fifty-three year old woman was admitted to the endoscopic unit of our institution because of a screening endoscopy.
The colonoscopy visually reveals a six-centimeter diameter tumor located in the proximal ascending colon.
The patient had a previous abdominal surgery for ovarian cysts and appendectomy twenty-six years before.
Laboratory tests were normal without anemia nor elevation of serum carcinome embryonic antigen.
A CT scan confirmed the presence of citizen versus totalis, with complete transposition of thoracic and abdominal organs and vessels.
Tumor was visible in the abdominal CT scan, while no clinically visible nodes nor secondary lesion were detected.
The radiologist evaluated the vessel in order to identify anomalies useful to plan the surgical treatment.
A three-dimensional reconstruction of the patient's vessels is displayed, highlighting key vascular landmarks for surgical planning.
The surgeon places four 8mm robotic trocars in the left quadrant and a 12mm assistant trocar in the right flank.

The surgeon identifies the ileocolic vessels and makes an incision in the peritoneum, opening the space between the rota and total fascia. Dissection proceeds through the vascular plane in a medial to lateral direction.
The left iliac artery and left ureter are visible, covered by the Girota fascia, within the surgical field.
The surgeon skeletonizes the ileocolic vessels at their origin from the superior mesenteric vessels, carefully exposing these vascular structures.
The surgeon clips and divides the ileocolic vein, followed by applying clips on the ileocolic artery and dividing the vessel.
The duodenum, pancreas, and Fredae fascia are clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to perform precise dissection of the anatomical plane, carefully separating tissue layers within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the last ileal loop and proceeds to coagulate the arched tracts using electrocautery.
The surgeon staples the last ileal loop, securing it in place as part of the colorectal procedure.
The surgeon clips and divides the right branch of the mid-colic artery, which is positioned on the left side in this case.
The surgeon sections the fomental vessels, achieving complete mobilization of the specimen, with visible anatomical structures such as the duodenum, pancreas, and fredae fascia.
The surgeon staples the transverse colon, securing the tissue with surgical staples.
The robotic platform is repositioned to facilitate the anastomosis without interference. A 3-0 silk stay suture is placed to ensure proper orientation of the bowel segments.

A two-centimeter opening is created in the transverse colon and small bowel. The surgeon performs a robotic hand-sewn anastomosis using a single-layer barbed-running suture to connect the two structures.
Silk sutures are placed to prevent traction on the anastomosis and to close the mesenteric defect. The greater omentum is positioned over the anastomosis for coverage.

The adoption of a robotic platform guarantees the ability to perform an intracorporeal and soon anastomosis. This technique has some advantages. It offers high accuracy, augmenting dexterity and magnifying the view of the surgeon.

It is a single operator technique, which involves the first operator without the need for coordination. It offers the opportunity to calibrate the anterotomy dimension and the cost of the suture is low.

The main disadvantages are the time needed to complete the anastomosis and the manipulation of the bowel with the robotic forceps. And compared to staple anastomosis, the robotic hand serum technique is more precise, it involves a single surgeon, it is more time-consuming, it requires more tissue manipulation, and it costs less.
Total operative time was hundred and seventy-five minutes and blood loss volume was twenty ml.

Patient started on a clear liquid diet on post-operative day one and a regular diet the next day. The first bowel movement was recorded on post-operative day one. The bowel opened on post-operative day three. Patient was discharged on post-operative day four.
No post-operative complications occurred in the next thirty days. The histopathological examination of the resected specimen confirmed the presence of a colorectal adenocarcinoma. Stage IIIb.
